meta.title: Dart & Flutter
meta.description: Review checklist for Dart and Flutter applications

items.text: Is state management scoped correctly (not over-global)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: State should be scoped to where it is needed. Global state (app-level providers) should only hold truly global concerns. Screen-local state should use local providers or StatefulWidget.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Over-global state causes unnecessary rebuilds, makes testing harder, and creates implicit dependencies between unrelated screens.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if providers are scoped too broadly. Does a single provider manage state for multiple unrelated features? Could it be split or scoped to a subtree?
baseContent.exampleComment: This provider manages both auth state and cart state. Split into separate providers so cart changes do not rebuild auth-dependent widgets.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
final authProvider = StateNotifierProvider<AuthNotifier, AuthState>((ref) => AuthNotifier());
final cartProvider = StateNotifierProvider<CartNotifier, CartState>((ref) => CartNotifier());

baseContent.keyTakeaway: One concern per provider. Scope as narrowly as possible.

items.text: Is state immutable, updated via copyWith or new instances?

baseContent.whatItMeans: State objects should be immutable. Updates create new instances via copyWith. Mutating state directly bypasses change detection and causes bugs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Flutter compares state objects by identity. If you mutate a field on an existing object, the framework does not detect the change and does not rebuild.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check state classes for mutable fields. Are updates done via state = state.copyWith(field: newValue) or by mutating fields directly?
baseContent.exampleComment: state.items.add(item) mutates the list in place. Flutter will not rebuild. Use state = state.copyWith(items: [...state.items, item]).

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never mutate state. Always create new instances with copyWith.

items.text: Are controllers and streams properly disposed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: TextEditingController, AnimationController, ScrollController, and StreamSubscription must be disposed in the dispose() method to prevent memory leaks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Undisposed controllers leak memory and can cause 'setState called after dispose' errors when callbacks fire after the widget is removed from the tree.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check StatefulWidget classes for controllers created in initState. Is dispose() overridden to clean them up?
baseContent.exampleComment: This TextEditingController is created in initState but never disposed. Add @override void dispose() { _controller.dispose(); super.dispose(); }.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every controller created in initState must be disposed in dispose().

items.text: Is the build method free of side effects?

baseContent.whatItMeans: build() can be called many times per frame. It must not make API calls, write to databases, navigate, or modify state. Side effects go in initState, callbacks, or effect hooks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Side effects in build() cause infinite loops (setState in build triggers rebuild triggers build), duplicate API calls, and navigation loops.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check build() methods for API calls, setState, Navigator.push, or any non-UI code. These belong in callbacks or lifecycle methods.
baseContent.exampleComment: This API call in build() fires on every rebuild. Move it to initState or a FutureProvider.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: build() returns widgets. Nothing else. Side effects go elsewhere.

items.text: Are const constructors used where possible?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Widgets with const constructors are canonicalized by the compiler. Identical const widgets share a single instance, avoiding unnecessary rebuilds.
baseContent.whyItMatters: const widgets are skipped during rebuilds because the framework knows they have not changed. This reduces build time and improves scrolling performance.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for widgets that could be const but are not. Enable the prefer_const_constructors lint. Look for new Text('...') that could be const Text('...').
baseContent.exampleComment: This SizedBox(height: 16) is recreated on every build. Make it const SizedBox(height: 16) to avoid the rebuild.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
Column(
  children: [
    SizedBox(height: 16),      // recreated every build
    Text('Hello'),             // recreated every build
    Icon(Icons.star),          // recreated every build
  ],
)

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use const everywhere you can. Enable the lint to catch misses.

items.text: Are large build methods broken into smaller widgets?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A build method over 50 lines should be split into smaller widget classes (not helper methods). Widget classes have their own Element and can skip rebuilds independently.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Helper methods rebuild with the parent every time. Extracted widget classes only rebuild when their own inputs change. This also improves readability.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for build methods over 50 lines. Check for _buildHeader, _buildBody helper methods that should be separate widgets.
baseContent.exampleComment: This 120-line build method has _buildHeader and _buildFooter helpers. Extract them into HeaderWidget and FooterWidget classes for independent rebuilds.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Extract widgets, not methods. Widget classes have independent rebuild scopes.

items.text: Are Keys used correctly for lists and conditional widgets?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When widgets in a list can be reordered, added, or removed, each needs a unique Key. Without keys, Flutter may reuse state from the wrong widget.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing keys in lists cause input fields to show wrong data after reorder, checkboxes to toggle the wrong item, and animations to glitch.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check ListView.builder and similar. Do items have ValueKey or ObjectKey? Check conditional widget swaps (if/else returning different widget types).
baseContent.exampleComment: This ListView has no keys on its items. Reordering the list will cause text fields to display the wrong user's data. Add ValueKey(user.id).

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
ListView.builder(
  itemCount: users.length,
  itemBuilder: (context, index) => UserTile(user: users[index]),
)

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
ListView.builder(
  itemCount: users.length,
  itemBuilder: (context, index) => UserTile(
    key: ValueKey(users[index].id),
    user: users[index],
  ),
)

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Key every item in a dynamic list. Use stable IDs, not indices.

items.text: Does the layout adapt to different screen sizes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use MediaQuery, LayoutBuilder, or Flex widgets to adapt layouts. Avoid hardcoded widths. Test on phones, tablets, and (if applicable) desktop.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Flutter runs on phones, tablets, web, and desktop. Hardcoded pixel values cause overflow on small screens and wasted space on large ones.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run on different screen sizes (or use the device preview plugin). Check for hardcoded widths, fixed-height containers, and non-scrollable overflowing layouts.
baseContent.exampleComment: This Container has width: 400, which overflows on small phones. Use ConstrainedBox with maxWidth or a fraction of MediaQuery.sizeOf(context).width.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use flexible layouts. Test on multiple screen sizes. Avoid hardcoded dimensions.

items.text: Does the app handle deep links correctly?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Deep links should navigate to the correct screen with proper state. The back button should navigate to a sensible parent, not exit the app.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Deep links from notifications, emails, and other apps are a primary entry point. Broken deep links frustrate users and hurt engagement.
baseContent.howToVerify: Test deep links with flutter run --route or adb for Android. Does the correct screen load? Does back navigation work?
baseContent.exampleComment: Opening the deep link /orders/123 shows a blank screen because the orders list is not loaded. Ensure the route handler loads required data.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Deep links must work standalone. Load required data in the route, not the parent.

items.text: Are routes guarded for authentication and permissions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Protected routes should redirect unauthenticated users to login. Admin routes should check permissions. Use GoRouter's redirect or equivalent.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without route guards, users can navigate directly to protected screens (via deep links or URL manipulation on web) without authentication.
baseContent.howToVerify: Try navigating to a protected route without logging in. Does it redirect to login? After login, does it return to the intended page?
baseContent.exampleComment: The /settings route is accessible without login via deep link. Add a redirect guard that checks auth state.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Guard protected routes. Redirect to login. Return after authentication.

items.text: Is WillPopScope/PopScope used correctly for unsaved changes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Forms with unsaved changes should warn before navigating away. PopScope (Flutter 3.16+) intercepts the back button and system back gesture.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Users accidentally lose form data when they swipe back or press the back button. A confirmation dialog prevents frustration.
baseContent.howToVerify: Fill out a form partially and press back. Is there a confirmation dialog? Check that PopScope.canPop is false when there are unsaved changes.
baseContent.exampleComment: This form has no back navigation guard. If the user accidentally swipes back, all entered data is lost. Add PopScope with a confirmation dialog.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Guard forms with unsaved changes. Use PopScope to intercept back navigation.

items.text: Are FutureBuilder and StreamBuilder handling all states (loading, error, data)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: FutureBuilder has four connection states: none, waiting, active, done. The builder must handle waiting (loading), error (snapshot.hasError), and data (snapshot.hasData).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing state handling causes blank screens (no loading indicator), unhandled errors (red error screen in debug), or null crashes (accessing data before it loads).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check FutureBuilder and StreamBuilder builders. Do they check snapshot.connectionState, snapshot.hasError, and snapshot.hasData?
baseContent.exampleComment: This FutureBuilder only handles the data case. It shows nothing while loading and crashes on error. Handle all states.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Handle loading, error, and data states. Never assume data is available.

items.text: Are Futures not recreated on every build?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Creating a Future inside build() means it is recreated on every rebuild, triggering new API calls each time. Store the Future in initState or a state variable.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A Future in build() causes infinite API calls: the call completes, triggers setState, which triggers build, which creates a new Future, ad infinitum.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check FutureBuilder. Is the future parameter an inline function call? It should reference a stored Future.
baseContent.exampleComment: FutureBuilder(future: fetchUser()) creates a new future on every build. Store it: late final _userFuture = fetchUser(); and use FutureBuilder(future: _userFuture).

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Store Futures in initState or a field. Never create them inline in build.

items.text: Are async errors caught and displayed to the user?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every async call can fail. Errors should be caught, shown to the user in a friendly way, and logged. Uncaught errors surface as red error screens or silent failures.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unhandled async exceptions crash the app (in debug mode, red error screen; in release, silent failure or freeze). Users see nothing useful.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check async calls for try-catch or .catchError. Is the error displayed to the user (SnackBar, dialog)? Is it logged?
baseContent.exampleComment: This network call has no error handling. If the API is down, the user sees a spinner forever. Add try-catch with a SnackBar for the error.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Catch every async error. Show a user-friendly message. Log the details.

items.text: Are async operations cancelled when the widget is disposed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When a widget is removed from the tree, pending async operations should be cancelled. Otherwise, callbacks try to update a disposed widget, causing errors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Calling setState after dispose throws an exception. Even without setState, completed but unneeded work wastes resources.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for async calls in StatefulWidget. Is there a mounted check before setState? Are StreamSubscriptions and Timers cancelled in dispose()?
baseContent.exampleComment: This setState in the .then callback will throw after the widget is disposed. Add if (!mounted) return; before setState.

codeExamples.label: Bad

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Check mounted before setState. Cancel subscriptions in dispose.

items.text: Is null safety used correctly without excessive null assertions (!)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Dart's sound null safety makes nullability explicit. The ! operator asserts non-null at runtime and throws if wrong. Prefer ?., ??, and proper null checks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Every ! is a potential runtime crash. The point of null safety is to catch null errors at compile time, not defer them to runtime.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for ! operators (excluding !=). For each, ask: can this actually be null? If yes, handle the null case. If no, refactor to make the type non-nullable.
baseContent.exampleComment: widget.onTap!() will crash if onTap is null. Use widget.onTap?.call() or check with if (widget.onTap != null).
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Minimize ! operators. Use ?., ??, and pattern matching for null handling.

items.text: Does the code follow Effective Dart style guidelines?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Follow Dart conventions: lowerCamelCase for variables/functions, UpperCamelCase for classes, snake_case for file names. Use analysis_options.yaml with recommended lints.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consistent style makes code readable and reduces review friction. The analyzer catches many issues automatically.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run dart analyze. Check for naming convention violations. Verify analysis_options.yaml enables flutter_lints or very_good_analysis.
baseContent.exampleComment: The file is named UserProfile.dart. Dart convention is user_profile.dart (snake_case for files).
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Follow Effective Dart. Enable strict analysis options. Run dart analyze in CI.

items.text: Are data classes using freezed or sealed classes for type safety?

baseContent.whatItMeans: freezed generates immutable data classes with copyWith, equality, and JSON serialization. Dart 3's sealed classes enable exhaustive pattern matching.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hand-written copyWith and equality are error-prone. Sealed classes with pattern matching ensure all cases are handled at compile time.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new data classes. Do they have manual copyWith or == implementations? Would freezed reduce boilerplate? Are sum types using sealed classes?
baseContent.exampleComment: This class has a manual copyWith with 12 parameters. Use freezed to generate it. Also, the result type would benefit from a sealed class.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use freezed for data classes. Use sealed classes for type hierarchies.

items.text: Are extension methods used appropriately for utility functions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Extension methods add functionality to existing types without inheritance. They are great for domain-specific utilities on String, DateTime, BuildContext, etc.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Extensions make code more readable (context.showSnackBar() vs ScaffoldMessenger.of(context).showSnackBar()). But overuse makes code harder to discover.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for utility functions that take a type as the first parameter. Would they read better as extensions? Check for overly broad extensions on Object or dynamic.
baseContent.exampleComment: formatCurrency(amount) would read better as amount.formatCurrency() via an extension on num.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use extensions for natural syntax. Avoid extending Object or basic types too broadly.

items.text: Are platform channels (MethodChannel) type-safe and error-handled?

baseContent.whatItMeans: MethodChannel calls between Dart and native code are stringly-typed. Use Pigeon for type-safe codegen. Always handle MissingPluginException for platforms that do not implement the channel.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Typos in channel names or method names cause silent failures. Missing platform implementations crash on web or desktop if not handled.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for raw MethodChannel usage. Could it use Pigeon? Is MissingPluginException handled? Are all supported platforms implemented?
baseContent.exampleComment: This MethodChannel call has no error handling. It will crash on web where the native plugin is not available. Handle PlatformException.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Pigeon for type safety. Handle MissingPluginException for cross-platform.

items.text: Are package versions constrained appropriately in pubspec.yaml?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use caret syntax (^1.2.3) for semver-compatible ranges. Pin exact versions only when necessary. Keep pubspec.lock committed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unconstrained versions (any) pull in breaking changes. Over-pinned versions prevent security patches. pubspec.lock ensures reproducible builds.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check pubspec.yaml for version constraints. Are they using ^? Is pubspec.lock committed? Run flutter pub outdated for stale dependencies.
baseContent.exampleComment: This dependency uses 'any' as the version constraint. Pin it to a specific range like ^2.0.0 to prevent unexpected breaking changes.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use caret syntax. Commit pubspec.lock. Run pub outdated regularly.

items.text: Is the code compatible with all target platforms (mobile, web, desktop)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Not all packages work on all platforms. dart:io is unavailable on web. Some plugins only support iOS and Android. Check platform compatibility before adding dependencies.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A package that works on mobile but not web will crash at runtime on web. Platform checks should happen at compile time or with conditional imports.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check pub.dev platform badges for new packages. Search for dart:io imports that need conditional imports for web. Test on all target platforms.
baseContent.exampleComment: This package only supports iOS and Android but the app also targets web. Use a conditional import or find a cross-platform alternative.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Check platform support before adding packages. Use conditional imports for platform-specific code.

items.text: Are widget tests written for key UI components?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Widget tests render components in isolation and verify their behavior. They are faster than integration tests and more meaningful than unit tests for UI code.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Widget tests catch layout issues, tap handler bugs, and state management problems without running on a device. They run in milliseconds.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for testWidgets calls for new or modified widgets. Do they cover tap interactions, state changes, and edge cases?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new LoginForm widget has no tests. Add widget tests that verify validation messages, button states, and successful submission.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Widget tests are Flutter's sweet spot. Fast, meaningful, and easy to write.

items.text: Are golden tests used for pixel-perfect UI verification?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Golden tests compare rendered widget output against reference images. They catch unintended visual regressions that other tests miss.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A 1px padding change or wrong color can slip through code review. Golden tests catch visual regressions automatically.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if visually complex widgets have golden tests. Run flutter test --update-goldens when intentionally changing appearance.
baseContent.exampleComment: This redesigned card component has no golden test. Add one to prevent future visual regressions.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Golden tests for visual components. Update goldens when the design intentionally changes.

items.text: Are dependencies mocked with Mockito or fakes for unit tests?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Unit tests should isolate the code under test by mocking or faking dependencies. Use @GenerateMocks with mockito, or write manual fakes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Tests that hit real APIs or databases are slow, flaky, and non-deterministic. Mocked dependencies make tests fast and reliable.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that test files mock external dependencies (API clients, databases). Verify build_runner has generated mock files for @GenerateMocks.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test calls the real API. Create a MockApiClient with Mockito and inject it into the class under test.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Mock external dependencies. Keep unit tests fast and deterministic.

items.text: Are Semantics labels provided for screen readers?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Interactive widgets and images need Semantics labels for TalkBack (Android) and VoiceOver (iOS). Use Semantics widget or semanticLabel parameter.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without semantic labels, screen reader users cannot understand or interact with the app. This excludes a significant portion of users.
baseContent.howToVerify: Enable TalkBack/VoiceOver and navigate the app. Are all interactive elements announced meaningfully? Use the Semantics Debugger overlay.
baseContent.exampleComment: This IconButton has no semantic label. Screen readers will announce 'button' with no context. Add semanticLabel: 'Delete item'.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
IconButton(
  icon: const Icon(Icons.delete, semanticLabel: 'Delete item'),
  onPressed: _deleteItem,
)

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Label all interactive widgets for screen readers. Test with TalkBack/VoiceOver.

items.text: Does the UI handle text scaling for large font settings?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Users with low vision set system font scaling to 1.5x or 2x. The app should not overflow, clip, or become unusable at large text sizes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Text scaling is an accessibility requirement. An app that breaks at 1.5x text size fails accessibility audits and excludes users who need larger text.
baseContent.howToVerify: Set the device text scale to 2x in accessibility settings. Navigate every screen. Check for text overflow, clipped buttons, and layout breaks.
baseContent.exampleComment: At 1.5x text scale, this button text overflows its container. Use Flexible or FittedBox to handle dynamic text sizes.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test at 2x text scale. Use flexible layouts that accommodate larger text.
